result:
I am the curator of the Museum of Leftover Weather. People assume this means clouds in boxes and rain on tap, neatly labeled: drizzle, downpour, deluge. They picture me sweeping sunshine into an envelope at closing time and sliding it under the door. I let them have their picture. What we keep is stranger than that, and quieter.

Leftover weather is what goes missing in transit. It’s thunder that was meant for 3:12 p.m. on a Tuesday but arrives no Tuesday at all. It’s the silver whisper of a coastal fog that strayed inland and lost its tide. It’s snow that fell in one backyard across town and didn’t make it over the low fence to the next. It’s light that held its breath for years waiting for the right window and then gave up, settling into the grain of a table.

We are in the old train depot, because the building understands arrivals and what to do with them. The arch of the ceiling collects echoes the way a mouth collects a name. The benches have been sealed against damp, but not too well; a museum of weather should swell a little in the summer and shrink in the winter. Volunteers stitched damp traps from torn timetables, and I catalog with graphite and fingerprints, because humidity lifts the neatness out of ink.

The exhibits are arranged in seasons that only make sense if you’ve ever waited for a particular wind. A hall for misdirected rains, pendant from the rafters in hand-blown bulbs that blur your reflection just enough to make you look like your own relative. A cabinet of crosswinds in shallow drawers, labeled by texture: burlap, nettle, satin. A velvet-lined case for snowscapes that never had the courage; you slide the panel back a few inches and feel the temperature fall across your knuckles like a hesitant apology.

Some weather is loud and refuses to be cased. We have a thunder that will not admit it is over. It sits in a room we have acoustically baffled and still finds a way to bruise the bones of your ear. On the hour, it paces; I go in with tea in a thick mug and read it the closing announcements like a bedtime story. It humors me by quieting at the line about umbrellas.

People bring their private fronts and accumulate in the lobby the way air finds a mountain. A man arrived with August heat sealed in a casserole dish, sweating through his shirt. It had been left in his mother’s kitchen on the day the power went out, and then fled into a drawer with the good napkins. A child walked across town with a shoebox, holding the lid down with both hands, eyes bright with mission. “I found this hail,” she said, breathless. It was peas and a few marbles pilfered from her brother’s jar; we accessioned the enthusiasm anyway, which is a weather of its own.

An old woman, elegant in a skirt that had seen rain, brought fog. Not the marine gray we all pretend to see in poetry, but a sharp white fog that makes headlights needy and hedges into an opinion. “It got stuck in my garden,” she said, fingers concerned at some remembered branch. “And then it forgot it was supposed to leave.” We keep it low to the ground. The local dogs come to sit in it when the day’s been too decisive.

There are rules. No whistling near the squalls. Keep your pockets zipped around dust devils and never feed them napkins. If you lift the corner of the tarp over the unsent thunderheads, you must commit to a story about a promise you once meant to keep. We find performance keeps the weather docile. It recognizes like for like.

Not all of it behaves. A breeze that reminds you of someone keeps sneaking out of its cage; every Tuesday we find it fondling the gift shop scarves and making the manager wistful. We do our best. I talk to the jars as I dust them, as if I might rub their names off; sometimes I tilt one, listen for a message like a seashell. I don’t open them. I am careful. I consider myself a steward of restraint.

We cannot accept everything. A heat dome once arrived, a letter with no return address and an airwave swelter that pressed the stamps against my palms. We took a sliver and smeared it on a spoon; it glowed for three days and then went to ground in the pantry. There are hurricanes we turn away because they come with more teeth than story, and the story is my jurisdiction. I write grants at a folding table in the back room; the air there is thick with the collective exhale of all the weather we’ve kept. I have learned to measure donations in condensation.

School trips come. Third graders bring questions like sparrows. Can you borrow rain? Can you pour it back where it belongs? If I climb inside this cloud, will my hair be curly like my aunt’s? Teachers shush. I do not offer science where wonder belongs, but I tell them truths that do not contradict. Borrowing weather is tricky, I say. People come in with jars of tears that they do not open. Sometimes we lend wind to help someone sail a small grief from one side of their day to the other. Sometimes we keep the jar, write a note inside the lid that says, Return with vegetables. The children solemnly promise to bring cucumbers.

The teenage meteorology club stood in front of the monsoon made entirely of unsent letters and took selfies with its posture. Behind the glass, the paper crinkled with a patience that could eat a house. They looked so sure of their instruments. I wanted to tell them that knowing is a good roof and a poor door.

This job found me the summer after my father died. He kept barometers the way some men keep coins, which is to say he kept time and weight but called it a hobby. He could tell what a day planned by the ache in his hand. “It’ll pass us by,” he’d say, frowning at the slack line of our horizon while we wanted thunder for the drama of it, for the roof drumming like applause. The day after the funeral, people brought casseroles that cooled too fast and a sky that had been holding itself all week like a person who refuses to cry in the restroom at work. The depot’s foundation drew me like a low note; I applied, I told the interviewers I was good with labels and patience. They left me alone with a test jar labeled “Late Afternoon” and I did not open it. I think that is what did it.

In the back, there is a shelf I don’t show on tours. Three jars no taller than my palm sit there, significant as knuckles. One is labeled First Snow, one is My Father’s Laugh, and one reads The Storm That Didn’t Come. You add these things last when you think you are done being a person and are ready to be a building.

The storm came into the museum on a Wednesday, folded into a postal sack that hummed. The carrier found me sweeping up a few misplaced leaves near the Vestibule of Breezes You Smelled on a Train and handed me the bag like an apology. I knew it before I undid the cord. It smelled like hot concrete and applause withheld. It smelled like that green hum in the air just before your name is called and you have to walk to the front alone.

We have a Yard of Controlled Releases out back, chain link and nettles and a sign that reads, in my hand, Please Sneeze Outside. I stood with the bag in my arms the way you hold a child who has slept through their stop. The sky pretended ignorance. I undid the knot. The storm lifted like anyone waking slowly, almost changing its mind. I said my father’s name the way you say a password you memorized years ago and have never once told another mouth.

What fell was not a deluge. It was not the drumroll I had wanted when I was ten with my ears ringing for it. It was a polite rain with manners, the sort that lingers on your eyelashes rather than slapping your cheek. Just enough to draw the oil up from the gravel and make the yard smell like a mechanic’s hands. I stood in it until my shirt clung and the taste of metal flicked against my teeth. I stood until it was over, which it decided in its own time, which was not long.

This is the note I made in the catalog: Storm That Didn’t Come, now partially spent. Residual sigh in bag. Unmeasurable return. Do not attempt to restock; do not attempt to forget.

We close at five but the museum does not really close. Weather presses its head against the glass and fog writes rude messages with its breath. I make my rounds with a flashlight and a voice that has not changed since I was a child counting out loud to fill a room. I touch the lids I am not going to open. I lock the gift shop and wave at the breeze that is trying to buy a scarf without currency. In the depot’s old clock, a draft keeps time. The building sighs. Somewhere, inside a drawer, a crosswind turns over, dreaming of wheat, of clothesline, of someone who will stand in a yard and let it lift the hair at the nape of their neck and call that, for one crooked second, forgiveness.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
